57.reason: The else clause only attaches to the last if (digit check), not to all three type checks. When the first or second if condition is true but the inner conditions fail, the else will incorrectly print '-', and when the first or second if succeeds and prints characters, the else may also execute incorrectly. Additionally, when the character before '-' is not a digit, the else will always print '-', even if the character is a letter that failed the inner checks.

When the first or second if condition is true but the inner conditions fail, the else will incorrectly print '-', and when the first or second if succeeds and prints characters, the else may also execute incorrectly. Additionally, when the character before '-' is not a digit, the else will always print '-', even if the character is a letter that failed the inner checks.
reasons.buggy-deepseek-chat-2: 1.0 , reason:  [Error: The else clause only attaches to the last if (digit check), not to all three type checks. When the first or second if condition is true but the inner conditions fail (e.g., different types or not increasing), the else will still execute and print '-', which is incorrect. The else should only print '-' when none of the three type conditions are met. Use a flag or restructure with else if to fix this logic.]
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-2: 0.5 , reason:  [This 'else' only pairs with the last 'if' (digit case), not the entire group of type checks. So if the character pair is lowercase or uppercase but invalid (e.g., d-a), it will fall through and print '-' incorrectly. Also, if none of the three type conditions match, it should print '-', but due to incorrect control flow, it may skip printing anything or print '-' in wrong cases.]

8.reason: [The function 'gets' is unsafe and has been removed from the C standard library since C11; many compilers no longer support it, leading to a compilation error. Use 'fgets' instead.]
